posts.caption:
Tennis and diamonds have shared a history for about a hundred years. Most people assume the connection is obvious. A sport played in pristine white, originally played on manicured grass, by some of the most photographed women in the world, would naturally feature jewellery. However, the relationship has an actual starting point, a gradual evolution, and, now, a second act in which jewellery brands aren’t just dressing tennis players, they are also designing with them.
The story begins like this: Chris Evert (@chrissieevert), who helped define an entire era of tennis along with her great rival Martina Navratilova (@martinanavratilova) in the 1970s and ’80s, lost her George Bedewi diamond bracelet mid-match. Evert refused to continue until the bracelet was found. When a reporter asked about it, she called it her “tennis bracelet”. While the details of this story have been corrected over time—the essence remains the same. It was the birth of a new jewellery category—sport and sparkle, fused by accident, forever.
At the link in bio, @drishtivij on the full history behind the tennis bracelet, and its evolution today.
posts.alt_text:
Even if you know nothing about Masaba Gupta (@masabagupta), at the very least you know that she’s famous. Some people know her simply as Masaba, founder of the fashion label House of Masaba (@houseofmasaba) that, for the last 17 years, has been making playfully printed contemporary Indian clothing. Others know her as the star of the Netflix show ‘Masaba Masaba’. To the chronically online, she’s the powerful influencer, the person brands go to when they want a cool, articulate personality to represent them. The makeup-obsessed know her via LoveChild by Masaba (@lovechildbymasaba), the beauty brand she started in 2021, which has taken her obsession with makeup and turned it into products built specifically around Indian skin tones.

Yet, as she continues to do it all, there is a change underway. As we walk out of her office, Masaba casually drops a piece of news. “I’m moving away from ready-to-wear entirely. I think spring/summer 2026 is the last one we’ll do. The focus is going to be on occasion and bridalwear.” It’s a pretty big declaration, and a timely one, considering that later this month Masaba will be a part of the FDCI Hyundai India Couture Week in New Delhi. Right now, it feels like Masaba is moving into a new era, one where she’s more of a founder and businesswoman than a niche fashion designer.

At the link in bio, @butoolbegum spends the day in the company of the enterprising designer, business owner, and new mom.

posts.caption:
Paris Haute Couture Week isn’t just about what’s on the runway anymore. The front row has become a conversation in itself, and Sudha Reddy’s appearances across Giorgio Armani Privé, Elie Saab and Manish Malhotra’s couture debut made for an interesting study in dressing for the house you’re sitting at.
For her second time around at the couture week, instead of relying on one signature formula, each look of Reddy’s reflected a different fashion language—Armani’s understated precision, Elie Saab’s romantic craftsmanship and Manish Malhotra’s sculptural Indian couture—all finished with a $30 million jewellery-suite that made a stunning statement.
Swipe through as we decode each front-row look, the standout details and, yes, the jewels that came with them.
—
#SudhaReddy #ParisHauteCoutureWeek #Ad #ParisFashionWeek

posts.alt_text:
“It would be unfair, even inaccurate, to deny the scale of work and craftsmanship on display. Malhotra, the irrepressible Bollywood-stylist-turned-couturier, has always understood the emotional charge of clothes, especially those designed for the grand entrance, the close-up, the wedding album and the cinematic reveal. But what was hard to ignore was how closely his work leaned into every cliché in Indian fashion’s new global couture playbook. The cocoon structure, overblown volume, bustiers and breastplates, exaggerated shoulders, embroidered armour, and a language overawed by technique.” More on Manish Malhotra’s much-anticipated Paris Haute Couture Week debut at link in bio.

🖋️ @kimidangor 

@manishmalhotraworld 
#thevofashion #manishmalhotra #parisfashion

posts.caption: Temple sculptures belong to everyone.... sounds very comforting. History otherwise says that they didn't even belong to the people who made them. Temple sculptures were created by hereditary artisan communities such as the Vishwakarmas in Hindu caste society. Brahmin priest had to conduct rituals to purify the sculpture from the impure touch of the lower caste sculptor - the sculpture then became deity. These sculptors although recorded occasionally, never got much credit for the art while ruling-class kings, patrons, and dynasties were memorialised through inscriptions and history. That pattern never disappeared. Today, those same sculptures are reborn through the embroidery and textile skills preserved and passed on by hereditary artisan communities, yet the authorship, prestige, media attention, and profits flow to designers, brands, and other elite cultural curators instead of the people whose labour and knowledge make the work possible. Indian couture lacks big spectacle or vision once all that embroidery & craft is stripped away. The craft is visible. It's the craftsperson who is invisible. They were invisible back when they made those sculptures in temples or now when they embroided it on textiles. This is the cultural appropriation & credit theft upper caste Indians will be dead silent about.

posts.alt_text: In an industry where the typical sample is a size zero, weight has always been central to a model’s success. But now, GLP-1 weight-loss medications offer a tempting shortcut to thinness. Models, modeling agents, casting directors, and designers are all noting a startling return to skinniness on the runway. “Models have definitely gotten smaller,” says @_ZoeLatta_ of the New York fashion brand @Eckhaus_Latta. “I think it's a combination of GLP-1s and a pendulum swing to extreme thinness being an idealized look again.” At the link in our bio, @RorySat explores how semaglutides and more GLP-1s have affected the modeling industry as we know it.

A summer well spent might look like this: lounging in the sun, a few more cocktails than usual, a late-night gelato, and an even later-night dancing session. And though this certainly sounds great—and can be, in moderation—your lymphatic system takes a hit. The first, and most viral, solution? A lymphatic drainage massage. However, there are plenty of other things you can do to help your lymphatic system without a professional. At the link in bio, learn more from massage therapist @Tehani_Leprieur as she breaks down some of her top recommendations.

posts.caption:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.
She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”
Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.
For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile.
Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

posts.caption: The latest stop on this odyssey? Le Grand Rex in Paris. The cast of Christopher Nolan’s forthcoming cinematic epic stepped out for the Parisian premiere, embracing Greek mythology sartorial codes. At the link in bio, get the details on Hathaway’s look for the occasion.
posts.alt_text:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.

She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”

Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.

For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile. 

Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

posts.caption:
Niki Patel (@itsnikirana) and Dip Rana (@kristinpiteophoto) booked the venues for their wedding in Antigua, Guatemala, before they had ever set foot in the city, across the region's historic locations, with three of the four events hosted within the UNESCO World Heritage city’s restored ruins. The wedding ceremony itself took place inside the ruins of Convento Santa Clara. Initially drawn to ivory bridalwear, Patel changed direction after seeing the venue in person. “I felt like I would blend too much into the beige stone of the ruins,” she says. She then worked with Pooja & Keyur on a minimal red lehenga with zardozi borders and a tissue dupatta, paired with her mother’s chooda and gold bangles inherited from her late grandmother. To see their wedding up-close, head to the link in bio.
Image credits:
#VogueWeddings

posts.caption: Size inclusivity in menswear remains largely static across #MilanFashionWeek and #ParisFashionWeek, with this season also welcoming more polarized body ideals. Alongside the continued dominance of straight-size casting, there was a sharp focus on extreme physiques, from "protein chic" and hyper-muscular builds shaped by looksmaxxing, to the return of ultra-slim "slender man" silhouettes. We found that of the thousands of looks presented across Milan and Paris SS27 shows, only 0.2% were shown on plus-size models, remaining unchanged since FW26. Tap the link in bio for a break down of the full report.
posts.alt_text: The walk-on look has arrived as tennis’s answer to the NBA tunnel walk. @NaomiOsaka’s custom kimono-inspired gown by @_HanaYagi at Wimbledon last week marked a new era of pre-match style — one where fashion becomes part of the game. From Osaka’s custom @Nike kits by @RobertWun to @Taylor_Fritz’s bespoke white @Boss suit, players are turning the walk onto court into a moment of self-expression, storytelling, and spectacle. At the link in bio, @LadyMollyElizabeth explores how, as more players embrace the pre-match style moment, more brands are tapping in.

Looking to Wimbledon regulars like Sienna Miller, Alexa Chung, and Catherine, Princess of Wales for inspiration—and pulling from the season’s most coveted new shopping arrivals—shop five winning outfits to wear to Wimbledon and beyond at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
What do you think of when you picture rubber? Surgical gloves? BDSM? An eraser? All are certainly true, but one thing that’s probably missing from your mind’s eye is images of this season’s couture. Yet, the thoroughly untraditional material cropped up—literally and figuratively—across the fall 2026 couture collections. From Schiaparelli to Jean Paul Gaultier and Robert Wun, tap the link in bio to read more on why latex is turning into one of fashion's next it-materials.

posts.caption: Earlier today in Paris, designer Pierpaolo Piccioli unveiled his first-ever fall 2026 couture collection for Balenciaga. And while Piccioli’s couture clothing delivered plenty of interesting offerings, all eyes were set on the label’s couture-ified City bags that were carried down the catwalk. Piccioli’s take on the classic City? Covering it in shimmering crystals. Get all the details on the couture version of the beloved Y2K staple at the link in bio. Once again, the dream team of Jacky Marshall (@jackyblue__)and @bluefarrier have picked up their brushes—and cameras—to document the couture and their processes for Vogue Runway. First up for fall 2026: @Schiaparelli and @IrisvanHepen, houses at which material exploration was pushed to new limits. Said Daniel Roseberry who used silicone in his Schiaparelli collection: “It was a total surrender to an unknown creative process, even on the materialization.” Iris van Herpen became the first designer on record to create a garment using plasma; in so doing, Amy Verner reported, she “has now proven that the immaterial can be material.”
posts.alt_text: Sweeping shapes, retina-reverberating color, and volume for miles: Pierpaolo Piccioli was showing the best of himself in his first haute couture collection for Balenciaga.  Read the full review from @sarahmower_ at the link in bio. Photographs by @styledumonde
